Turing pioneered the idea of Artificial Intelligence In the 1950s, Turing continued to theorise about the potential of computers in the future.

As a result of this work, Turing is now considered to be one of the fathers of machine learning and what we now know as artificial intelligence or AI. He also devised what has become known as the ‘Turing Test’, where he proposed a game in which, by asking only written questions and studying written answers, a player had to determine whether the answers were coming from a human or a machine.
The question of whether a machine can, or indeed has, passed this test is widely debated today.

A pioneer in computational science When Turing came to the Princeton Graduate School in 1936 after studies at King's College, Cambridge, he had already written the seminal paper, "On Computable Numbers."

In that paper, Turing described simple abstract computational devices that his Princeton adviser Alonzo Church'24 *27 — the renowned mathematician, logician, and philosopher — later coined as “Turing machines.”

Alan Turing was a brilliant original thinker.
Formally a mathematician, in his lifetime he studied and wrote papers over a whole spectrum of subjects, from philosophy and psychology through to physics, chemistry and biology.

He graduated from Cambridge in Mathematics in 1934, was a fellow at Kings College for two years, during which he wrote his famous paper which introduced the Turing Machine, went to Princeton for two years to do a Ph.D.
, and returned to Kings for a year.
Here he made a major contribution to the battle to decode the German Enigma encoding's, designing the "Bombe", though he was not directly involved with the later Colossus project.
In the summer of 1949 he was instrumental in acquiring paper tape equipment and assisted Dai Edwards in attaching it to the Mark 1.

However, the main formal contribution Turing made to the Mark 1 project was that he worked on providing the early software requirements for the Manchester Mark 1, with the full time help of Cicely Popplewell, and wrote the first programming manual for the Ferranti Mark 1.
However Turing was still a keen user of the computer as a tool for his research interests, which now turned to "morphogenesis", the theory of growth and form in biology.
And he was was always ready informally to help out other programmers of the Mark 1 with their problems.

During World War II, Turing worked for the Government Code and Cypher School at Bletchley Park, Britain's codebreaking centre that produced Ultra intelligence.
After the war, Turing worked at the National Physical Laboratory, where he designed the Automatic Computing Engine, one of the first designs for a stored-program computer.

Turing was in large part responsible not only for the concept of computers, incisive theorems about their powers, and a clear vision of the possibility of computer minds, but also for the cracking of German ciphers during the Second World War.

Between the springs of 1935 and 1936, at the same time as Alonzo Church, Turing worked on the decidability of problems, starting from Gödel's incompleteness theorems.
In mid-April 1936, Turing sent Max Newman the first draft typescript of his investigations.

From September 1936 to July 1938, Turing spent most of his time studying under Church at Princeton University, in the second year as a Jane Eliza Procter Visiting Fellow.

On 4 September 1939, the day after the UK declared war on Germany, Turing reported to Bletchley Park, the wartime station of GC&CS. Like all others who came to Bletchley, he was required to sign the Official Secrets Act, in which he agreed not to disclose anything about his work at Bletchley, with severe legal penalties for violating the Act.
Specifying the bombe was the first of five major cryptanalytical advances that Turing made during the war.

The bombe searched for possible correct settings used for an Enigma message (i.e., rotor order, rotor settings and plugboard settings) using a suitable crib: a fragment of probable plaintext.

For each possible setting of the rotors (which had on the order of 10 19 states, or 10 22 states for the four-rotor U-boat variant), the bombe performed a chain of logical deductions based on the crib, implemented electromechanically.
The first bombe was installed on 18 March 1940.

In 1948, Turing was appointed reader in the Mathematics Department at the University of Manchester.
A year later, he became deputy director of the Computing Machine Laboratory, where he worked on software for one of the earliest stored-program computers—the Manchester Mark 1.

Turing wrote the first version of the Programmer's Manual for this machine, was elected to membership of the Manchester Literary and Philosophical Society, and was recruited by Ferranti as a consultant in the development of their commercialised machine, the Ferranti Mark 1.

During this time, he continued to do more abstract work in mathematics, and in "Computing Machinery and Intelligence", Turing addressed the problem of artificial intelligence, and proposed an experiment that became known as the Turing test, an attempt to define a standard for a machine to be called" intelligent".

In'Computing Machinery and Intelligence', a foundational 1950 article on artificial intelligence, he proposed an imitation game to see if a computer could fool a person into thinking it was human—now famous as the'Turing Test'.
In 1968 the Association of Computing Machinery created the Turing Award in his name, regarded as the Nobel prize of computing.
From the 1970s Bletchley Park's wartime work was declassified and Turing's vital role uncovered.
Books, plays, postage stamps and television programmes celebrated Turing's life.
His statue sits on a park bench near Canal Street.
Cementing Turing's status as an iconic British figure, the Bank of England announced him as the face of the new £50 note in 2019, in a ceremony held right here at the Science and Industry Museum.

Yet it was in Manchester that Turing first encountered a real universal computer of the type he had imagined, and his life here left a significant legacy.
Born in London and educated in mathematics at the universities of Cambridge and Princeton, Alan Turing was only 24 years old when he published his idea for a'universal computing machine'.
In the 1930s, Turing’s computing machines had just been an idea, but Bletchley gave him the chance to develop real ones.
Building on work started by Polish codebreakers before their country was invaded, Turing and his colleague Gordon Welchman developed a machine to help the codebreakers: the Bombe.
In 1946, Turing designed the Automatic Computing Engine (ACE), a remarkably sophisticated design for a general-purpose stored program computer.
Pilot ACE, a simplified version of Alan Turing's ACE computer (1950).
The prototype Baby computer was far simpler than ACE, but it was the first truly universal computer and gave Turing his first chance to run a proper computer program, for long division.
The engineers were focussed on designing and building the things.
Alan Turing, Programmers'Handbook (1950).
Much of Turing's contribution to the Mark 1 was developing programming facilities.

There were no computer programming languages or operating systems at this point, so these were fundamental questions of deciding what operations the machine should do and finding ways to make them happen.
In 1950 he also wrote one of the first computer manuals, the Programmers'Handbook for the Mark 1.
Programming the prototype Baby computer was a long and tricky business.
In the 1930s, Turing had imagined a computing machine controlled by symbols on paper tape.

Alongside mathematics, like investigating prime numbers, he was interested in computer games.
Turing's major research interest became computing biological problems.
Turing's role as Deputy Director of the Computer Laboratory was supposed to involve helping people use this incredible new machine for their computation problems.
In practice, the day-to-day task of helping beginners was not the best use of his talents and much of his time was spent considering much bigger questions about the nature of computers.
Computers were a fantastic and little understood new technology in the 1950s.
They were often called'electronic brains'and there was much discussion over their abilities.

The Ferranti Mark 1 wasn't powerful enough to run Turing's own chess program, but he influenced more successful efforts by Ferranti's Dietrich Prinz, who wrote a limited chess problem program, and Christopher Strachey, who got the Mark 1 to play draughts and write love letters.
However, for the day it was a remarkable machine.
Much of Turing's computing at Manchester was done on a Ferranti Mark 1, which was the commercial version of the Manchester Mark 1 Computer, which Turing also helped to develop.
Ferranti took the basic design of Manchester University's experimental computer and engineered it into a product for sale.
Customers included Stockport aircraft designer Avro and the atomic research centre at Aldermaston.
However, for the day it was a remarkable machine.
He was sentenced to probation with a course of hormone treatment—chemical castration.
Alan Turing, Computing Machinery and Intelligence (1950).
We can only see a short distance ahead, but we can see plenty there that needs to be done.
Aged just 41, Turing died just as the computer age was beginning.
In 1968 the Association of Computing Machinery created the Turing Award in his name, regarded as the Nobel prize of computing.

Scientists, celebrities, politicians and thousands of other people began to support a campaign for a Royal Pardon, writing letters, singing petitions and marching with placards at Pride parades.

Further reading A Hodges, Alan Turing: The Enigma (Burnett Books/Hutchinson, 1983) S Lavington, A History of Manchester Computers (Blackwell, 1975) S Lavington et al., Alan Turing and his Contemporaries: Building the world's first computers (BCS Learning & Development Limited, 2012) J Swinton, Alan Turing's Manchester (The History Press, 2022)
Alan Turing Artificial intelligence pioneer.
Turing did the earliest work on AI, and he introduced many of the central concepts of AI in a report entitled “Intelligent Machinery” (1948).
In fact, Turing and Church showed that even some purely logical systems, considerably weaker than arithmetic, have no effective decision method.

It was in the course of his work on the Entscheidungsproblem that Turing invented the universal Turing machine, an abstract computing machine that encapsulates the fundamental logical principles of the digital computer.

In 1936 Turing and Church independently showed that, in general, the Entscheidungsproblem problem has no resolution, proving that no consistent formal system of arithmetic has an effective decision method.
The Church-Turing thesis.

An important step in Turing’s argument about the Entscheidungsproblem was the claim, now called the Church-Turing thesis, that everything humanly computable can also be computed by the universal Turing machine.
The claim is important because it marks out the limits of human computation.
During the autumn of 1939 and the spring of 1940, Turing and others designed a related, but very different, code-breaking machine known as the Bombe.
The Bomba depended for its success on German operating procedures, and a change in those procedures in May 1940 rendered the Bomba useless.

By early 1942 the cryptanalysts at Bletchley Park were decoding about 39,000 intercepted messages each month, a figure that rose subsequently to more than 84,000 per month—two messages every minute, day and night.
At the end of the war, Turing was made an Officer of the Most Excellent Order of the British Empire (OBE) for his code-breaking work.
His design for the Automatic Computing Engine (ACE) was the first complete specification of an electronic stored-program all-purpose digital computer.
His earlier theoretical concept of a universal Turing machine had been a fundamental influence on the Manchester computer project from the beginning.

NPL lost the race to build the world’s first working electronic stored-program digital computer—an honour that went to the Royal Society Computing Machine Laboratory at the University of Manchester in June 1948.
After Turing’s arrival at Manchester, his main contributions to the computer’s development were to design an input-output system—using Bletchley Park technology—and to design its programming system.

Turing was a founding father of artificial intelligence and of modern cognitive science, and he was a leading early exponent of the hypothesis that the human brain is in large part a digital computing machine.
Turing proposed what subsequently became known as the Turing test as a criterion for whether an artificial computer is thinking (1950).
Turing used Manchester’s Ferranti Mark I computer to model his hypothesized chemical mechanism for the generation of anatomical structure in animals and plants.

Yet he died more than a year after the hormone doses had ended, and, in any case, the resilient Turing had borne that cruel treatment with what his close friend Peter Hilton called “amused fortitude.”

Although suicide cannot be ruled out, it is also possible that his death was simply an accident, the result of his inhaling cyanide fumes from an experiment in the tiny laboratory adjoining his bedroom.
By the early 21st century Turing’s prosecution for being gay had become infamous.
Last years.
In the midst of this groundbreaking work, Turing was discovered dead in his bed, poisoned by cyanide.
The official verdict was suicide, but no motive was established at the 1954 inquest.
Four years later Queen Elizabeth II granted Turing a royal pardon.
